Liaquat Ali alias Liaqu 3.Aman Ullah ) Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 (Muhammad Bilal alias Mithu Vs. The State.) Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 (Liaquat Ali alias Liaqu Vs. The State.) Criminal Appeal No. 111-J of 2018 (Aman Ullah Vs. The State.) Date of hearing: 06.09.2022. Appellants by: Mr. James Joseph, Advocate. State by: Mr. Muhammad Ali Shahab, Deputy Prosecutor General. Complainant by: Mehr Habib Ullah Girwah, Advocate. SADIQ MAHMUD KHURRAM, J. – Muhammad Bilal alias Mithu son of Abdul Jabbar, Liaquat Ali alias Liaqu son of Maqsood Ahmad and Aman Ullah son of Shah Muhammad (convicts) were tried alongwith Abdul Rauf son of Abdul Ghaffar and Munir Hussain son of Sardar Muhammad (both since acquitted), by the learned Additional Sessions Judge Layyah, in case FIR No. 561 of 2012 dated 10.10.2012 registered in respect of offences under sections 302, 396,460 and 412 P.P.C. at the Police Station Chowk Azam District Layyah for committing the Qatl-i-Amd of Tahir Majeed and Abdul Majeed (deceased) during dacoity. The learned trial court vide judgment dated 10.02.2018 convicted Muhammad Bilal alias Mithu son of Abdul Jabbar, Liaquat Ali alias Liaqu son of Maqsood Ahmad, and Aman Ullah son of Shah Muhammad (convicts) and sentenced them as under: 2 Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 111-J of 2018. Muhammad Bilal alias Mithu son of Abdul Jabbar: i) Death on two counts under section 302(b) P.P.C. as Tazir for committing Qatl-i-Amd of Abdul Majeed and Tahir Majeed (deceased) and directed to pay Rs.100,000/- as compensation under section 544-A, Cr.P.C. to the legal heirs of each of the deceased, in case of default thereof, the convict was further directed to undergo six months of simple imprisonment for each default.

Rs.100,000/- as compensation under section 544-A, Cr.P.C. to the legal heirs of each of the deceased, in case of default thereof, the convict was further directed to undergo six months of simple imprisonment for each default. The convict was ordered to be hanged by his neck till dead; ii) Imprisonment for life under section 460 P.P.C. iii) Rigorous Imprisonment for ten years under section 392 P.P.C. and directed to pay fine of Rs.50,000/- and in case of default thereof, the convict was further directed to undergo six months of simple imprisonment for each default. The convicts were extended the benefit available under Section 382-B of Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898.All the sentences awarded to the convicts were ordered to run concurrently by the learned trial court. Abdul Rauf son of Abdul Ghaffar and Munir Hussain son of Sardar Muhammad, the co-accused of the convicts, were however acquitted by the learned trial court. 2. Feeling aggrieved, Muhammad Bilal alias Mithu son of Abdul Jabbar (convict) lodged Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 through jail assailing his conviction and sentences. Liaquat Ali alias Liaqu son of Maqsood Ahmad (convict) lodged Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 through jail assailing his conviction and sentences. Aman Ullah son of Shah Muhammad(convict) lodged Criminal Appeal 111-J of 2018 through jail assailing his conviction and sentences. The learned trial court submitted Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 under section 374 Cr.P.C. seeking confirmation or otherwise of the sentences of death awarded to the appellants namely Muhammad Bilal alias Mithu son of Abdul Jabbar, Liaquat Ali alias Liaqu son of Maqsood Ahmad and Aman Ullah son of Shah Muhammad (appellants). We intend to dispose of the Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018, 4 Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 111-J of 2018.

We intend to dispose of the Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018, 4 Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 111-J of 2018. Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018, Criminal Appeal 111-J of 2018 and the Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 through this single judgment. 3. Precisely the facts necessary, as stated by Asif Majeed (PW-17), the complainant of the case, are as under: - “My residential house is situated near private Al-Madina Grammar Public School Chowk Azam. On 10.10.2012 at about 2.00 am (night) as usual, 1 along with my wife and a minor daughter were sleeping in our bedroom. Meanwhile, three persons armed with pistols entered our bedroom and at that time, light of my room was on. I was awaken by the aforesaid persons and saw that one of the accused was of elongated face,middle height, smart body aged about 25/26, have increased shave, wearing Khaki coloured clothes. Amongst the aforesaid accused, another was of smart body, middle aged, with round face, long hairs, having "maang" from the center, without beard and moustaches aged about 18/20 years, wearing blue coloured clothes, and the third one was of round face, middle height, small moustaches, aged about 22/23 years wearing almond coloured clothes. As I arouse, one of them made a pistol blow at the back side of my head, the other two accused pulled out string of my Shalwar and tied my legs and they also tied my hands with my shirt and I was caused to be laid on the bed while facing downward, one of them took out mobile Nokia-6120 containing SIM No.0334/6962291 from pocket of my shirt and also forcibly removed gold ring from the hand of my wife Mst. Sajida Afzal. On our hue and cry, my elder brothers Tahir Majeed and Kashif Majeed also woke up and came t (sic) at that time towards my room and they tried to apprehend the accused persons.

Sajida Afzal. On our hue and cry, my elder brothers Tahir Majeed and Kashif Majeed also woke up and came t (sic) at that time towards my room and they tried to apprehend the accused persons. Meanwhile, 1 also untied myself with the help of my wife. One of the accused made a fire with pistol which hit Tahir Majeed at his abdomen in the hall room of my house, another accused made a pistol fire which landed on the thigh of Tahir Majeed who fell down on the ground then and there. On hearing the fires and the noise, my father Abdul Majeed also came out of the bedroom into the courtyard of the house and the third accused tried to launch an attack upon my father but my father took out small hatchet lying in the courtyard and made a hatchet blow at the said accused which landed at left arm of the accused upon which the said accused made pistol fire at my father Abdul Majeed which hit him on his chest and my father fell down on the ground. Second fire was also made by the same accused which landed on the back of my father Abdul Majeed, On hearing the firing and our hue and cry, Nadeem Abbas, Zafar Iqbal PWs and other neighbours attracted at the spot but all the three accused persons while making firing decamped from the spot. We took care of Tahir Majeed and Abdul Majeed but they had succumbed to the injuries. I and other PWs have seen the accused persons. We put both the dead bodies in a private dalla and escorted to the RHC Chowk Azam. All the accused with their common intention in order to commit robbery/dacoity snatched my mobile phone and gold ring of my wife forcibly and during this, they murdered my brother and father. On receiving the information of the occurrence, police came in the hospital where I made statement Exh PC, before the police which was reduced into writing by the police and was read over to me. 1 signed the same in token of its correctness.

All the accused with their common intention in order to commit robbery/dacoity snatched my mobile phone and gold ring of my wife forcibly and during this, they murdered my brother and father. On receiving the information of the occurrence, police came in the hospital where I made statement Exh PC, before the police which was reduced into writing by the police and was read over to me. 1 signed the same in token of its correctness. 5 Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 111-J of 2018. Police inspected the spot in my presence as well as in presence of Kashif Majeed on 10.10.2012 secured blood with the help of cotton from underneath the dead body of Tahir Majeed sealed it into parcel. Police also collected blood with the help of cotton from underneath the dead body of Abdul Majeed and sealed it into parcel. Both parcels were taken into possession vide recovery memo Exh.PAA attested by me and Kashif Majeed. Police also collected three live bullets from one point P.29/1-3 and two live bullets from other point P-30/1-2 of pistol 30-bore and took the same into possession vide recovery memo Exh.PBB attested by me and Kashif Majeed. Police also collected 6 crime empties of pistol 30-bore P.31/1-6 from one point and four crime empties of pistol 30-born P.32/1-4 which were sealed into parcel and taken into possession vide recovery memo Exh.PCC attested by me and Kashif Majeed PW. Police also collected string of Shalwar P.33 with which my legs were tied up and a pair of shoe of Calza P.34 left by the accused at the spot and the same was taken into possession vide recovery memo Exh.PDD attested by me and Kashif Majeed. On the same day, the police also collected blood stained small hatchet P.35, sealed into parcel, and took the same into possession vide recovery memo Exh.PE attested by me and Kashif Majeed: PW. My statement was also recorded by the Investigating Officer u/s 161 Cr.P.C. On 18.12.2012, 1 alongwith my brother: Kashif Majeed and wife Mst. Sajida Afzal went to Central Jail, Dera Ghazi Khan where under the supervision of learned Area Magistrate we all the PWs turn by turn identified the accused of this occurrence.

On 18.12.2012, 1 alongwith my brother: Kashif Majeed and wife Mst. Sajida Afzal went to Central Jail, Dera Ghazi Khan where under the supervision of learned Area Magistrate we all the PWs turn by turn identified the accused of this occurrence. I identified that the accused, who amongst the accused persons, had made first fire at Tahir Majeed was Amanullah, the accused present in the court, whose name was subsequently told to me. The accused, who had made second fire at Tahir Majeed was Liaqat Ali, the accused present in the court, whose name was subsequently told to me. The accused, who had made two fires at my father Abdul Majeed was Bilal alias Mithu, the accused present in the court, whose name was also subsequently told to me. In this way, we all the three PWs identified the accused who had committed this occurrence on the fateful night. On 20.12.2012, I went to the police station alongwith my wife Mst. Sajida Afzal and police showed me mobile phone Nokia-6120, stated to be recovered from the accused Muhammad Bilal, which I identified to be owned by me which was snatched by the accused persons from me on fateful night. My statement in this regard was also recorded by the Investigating Officer.’ 4. After the formal investigation of the case report under section 173 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 was submitted before the learned trial court and the appellants namely Muhammad Bilal alias Mithu son of Abdul Jabbar, Liaquat Ali alias Liaqu son of Maqsood Ahmad and Aman Ullah son of Shah Muhammad along with their co-accused namely Abdul Rauf son of Abdul Ghaffar and Munir Hussain son of Sardar Muhammad (both since acquitted), were sent to face trial. 6 Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 111-J of 2018. The learned trial court framed the charge against the accused on 14.06.2013 to which the accused pleaded not guilty and claimed trial. 5.

After the formal investigation of the case report under section 173 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 was submitted before the learned trial court and the appellants namely Muhammad Bilal alias Mithu son of Abdul Jabbar, Liaquat Ali alias Liaqu son of Maqsood Ahmad and Aman Ullah son of Shah Muhammad along with their co-accused namely Abdul Rauf son of Abdul Ghaffar and Munir Hussain son of Sardar Muhammad (both since acquitted), were sent to face trial. 6 Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 111-J of 2018. The learned trial court framed the charge against the accused on 14.06.2013 to which the accused pleaded not guilty and claimed trial. 5. The prosecution in order to prove its case got statements of as many as nineteen witnesses recorded. The ocular account of the case was furnished by Sajida Afzal (PW-16) and Asif Majeed (PW-17). Haji Muhammad ASI, (PW-1) stated that on 10.10.2012, he escorted the dead bodies of the deceased to the hospital and also received the last worn clothes of both the deceased from the Medical Officer. Nazar Abbas, Patwari (PW-2) prepared the scaled site plan of the place of occurrence (Exh.PB). Abdul Hameed, SI (PW-4) stated that on 10.10.2012, he recorded the formal F.I.R (Exh.PC/1). Muhammad Afzal (PW-7) stated that on 10.10.2012, he identified the dead bodies of both the deceased at the time of their post mortem examinations. Muhammad Afzal (PW-7) stated that on 10.10.2012, he identified the dead bodies of both the deceased at the time of their post mortem examinations. Sagheer Ahmad 779/C (PW-8) stated that on 13.12.2012, the appellant namely Muhammad Bilal alias Mithu got recovered a mobile phone device (P-17) and another mobile phone device (P-18), whereas on the same day the appellant namely Aman Ullah got 7 Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 111-J of 2018. recovered a mobile phone device (P-19), whereas on 26.12.2012, the appellant namely Liaquat Ali alias Liaqu got recovered a pistol (P-20) having two bullets (P- 21/1-2), whereas on 27.12.2012, the appellant namely Muhammad Bilal alias Mithu got recovered a pistol (P-22) and a live bullet (P-23) and on 27.12.2012, the appellant namely Aman Ullah got recovered a pistol (P-24) and three live bullets (P-25/1-3). Ijaz Ali, ASI (PW-10) stated that on 10.10.2012, the Investigating Officer of the case, handed over to him two sealed parcels said to contain blood stained cotton, one sealed parcel said to contain a hatchet, two sealed parcels said to contain empty shells of bullets, one sealed parcel said to contain live bullets, a shoe, a string of shalwar, six casts of foot prints and the last worn clothes of the deceased and on 21.10.2012, he handed over the said parcels to Muzammil Hussain, ASI (PW-3) for their onward transmission to the office of the Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore, however, the said parcels were returned and subsequently on 16.12.2012, the two sealed parcels said to contain the empties were handed over to Liaquat Ali 70/C (PW-5) for their onward transmission to the office of the Punjab Forensic Science Agency, Lahore. Muhammad Afzal, (PW-11) stated that on 10.10.2012, he procured six casts of footprints of the accused from the place of occurrence which were taken into possession through recovery memo (Exh.PZ). Abdul Waheed (PW-12) and Abdul Khaliq (PW-13) stated that on 20.12.2012, 8 Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 111-J of 2018. Abdul Rauf (since acquitted), the co-accused of the appellants, confessed before them of having committed the occurrence with the appellants. Muhammad Sharif (PW-14) and Abdul Hameed (PW-15) stated that on 21.12.2012, Munir Hussain (since acquitted), the co-accused of the appellants, confessed before them of having committed the occurrence with the appellants. Shamas Husnain Khan Sial, Magistrate (PW-18) got conducted the test identification parade proceedings on 18.12.2012 held to establish the identities of the appellants and prepared his report (Exh.PGG). Muhammad Afzal, SI (PW-19) investigated the case from 10.10.2012 till 09.02.2013, arrested the appellants namely Muhammad Bilal alias Mithu and Aman Ullah on 13.12.2012 and arrested the appellant namely Liaquat alias Liaqu on 17.12.2012 and detailed the facts of his investigation in his statement before the learned trial court. 6. The prosecution got Dr. Muhammad Tariq (PW-9) examined who, on 10.10.2012, was posted as Medical Officer at RHC Chowk Azam and on the same day conducted the postmortem examination of the dead body of Tahir Majeed son of Abdul Majeed (deceased). Dr. Muhammad Tariq (PW-9), on examining the dead body of the deceased namely Abdul Majeed son of Haji Sher Muhammad, observed as under:- “EXTERNAL INJURIES 1. 1 cm X 1 cm circular wound at abdomen, 5 cm above from amblicus (sic) and 3 cm left lateral to the mid line.

Dr. Muhammad Tariq (PW-9), on examining the dead body of the deceased namely Abdul Majeed son of Haji Sher Muhammad, observed as under:- “EXTERNAL INJURIES 1. 1 cm X 1 cm circular wound at abdomen, 5 cm above from amblicus (sic) and 3 cm left lateral to the mid line. Blackening was not present. Margins were inverted. Blood was oozing from the wound and it was a wound of entry. 2. 1.5 cm X 1 cm oval wound at the back, 4 cm right lateral to the mid line, 2 cm above the iliac crest. Margins were everted. It was the exit wound of injury No.1 3. A lacerated wound 1 cm x 1 cm at the lateral aspect of middle of left buttock. Margins were inverted. Blackning was present. It was a wound of entry by a second fire. 9 Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 111-J of 2018. 4. 1.5 cm X 1 cm lacerated wound at the posterior aspect of left buttock 8 cm below from the left iliac crest. Margins were everted. It was exit wound of injury No.3.......................................................................... In my opinion, the injuries were caused by firearm. Cause of death was injury to abdomen by firearm that resulted in excessive hemorrhage followed by shock and death. Time elapsed between injuries and death was immediately whereas between death and post-mortem was about four hours..” Dr. Muhammad Tariq (PW-9), on 10.10.2012, also conducted the postmortem examination of the dead body of Abdul Majeed son of Haji Sher Muhammad (deceased). Dr. Muhammad Tariq (PW-9), on examining the dead body of the deceased namely Abdul Majeed son of Haji Sher Muhammad, observed as under:- “An abrasion of 1 cm X 1 cm present on the middle of skull Blood was oozing out from mouth. External injuries were as under: 1. A circular wound 1 cm X 1 cm in front of chest, in mid line, 3 cm below sternal notch. Blackening was present. Margins were inverted. It was entry wound.

Margins were inverted. It was entry wound. Fracture of sternum at the site of injury was present. 2. An oval shaped wound of 1.5 cm X 1.5 cm at the right lateral aspect of middle of neck, 4 cm below from right ear pinna. Margins were everted. Blood was oozing, communicating with injury No.1 and it was exit wound of injury No.1. 3. A lacerated wound 1 cm X 1 cm at the lower end of scapula 6 cm right lateral to the mid line and it was skin deep......................... In my opinion, injuries were ante-mortem caused by firearm.The cause of death was injury to right lung and right carotid vessel that resulted in excessive hemorrhage followed by shock and death. Death occurred immediately after sustaining the injuries. However, duration between death and post-mortem was about 5 and half hours.” 10 Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 111-J of 2018. Dr. Muhammad Tariq (PW-9) added that on 10.10.2012 he also examined Asif Majeed (PW-17),however no Medico Legal Examination Certificate was issued as Asif Majeed (PW-17) was not willing to get his Medico Legal Examination Certificate prepared. Dr. Muhammad Tariq (PW-9) further stated that on 27.12.2012 he also examined the appellant namely Muhammad Bilal alias Mithu son of Abdul Jabbar and on examining the appellant namely Muhammad Bilal alias Mithu, he observed as under:- “1. A scar of 03 X 0.3 cm of healed wound at the medial aspect of palmer side of left hand and wrist joint 2. A scar of 1 X 0.5 cm of healed wound at the palmer aspect of metacarpophalyngeal (sic) joint of left index finger 3. A scar of 07 cm X 1 cm of healed wound at the antrolateral aspect of right thigh, 12 cm below from the right anterior superior iliac spine.

A scar of 1 X 0.5 cm of healed wound at the palmer aspect of metacarpophalyngeal (sic) joint of left index finger 3. A scar of 07 cm X 1 cm of healed wound at the antrolateral aspect of right thigh, 12 cm below from the right anterior superior iliac spine. All the wounds were liner so these may be due to sharp edged weapon but depth and duration of injuries cannot be assessed at the time of examination.” 7. After the closure of prosecution evidence, the learned trial court examined the appellants namely Muhammad Bilal alias Mithu son of Abdul Jabbar, Liaquat Ali alias Liaqu son of Maqsood Ahmad and Aman Ullah son of Shah Muhammad under section 342 Cr.P.C. and in answer to question why this case against you and why the PWs have deposed against you, the appellants namely Muhammad Bilal alias Mithu son of Abdul Jabbar, Liaquat Ali alias Liaqu son of Maqsood Ahmad and Aman Ullah son of Shah Muhammad replied that they were innocent and had been falsely involved in the case due to suspicion. The appellants namely Muhammad Bilal alias Mithu son of Abdul Jabbar, Liaquat Ali alias Liaqu son of Maqsood Ahmad and Aman Ullah son of Shah Muhammad further stated that they 12 Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 111-J of 2018. had not committed any offence and were not involved in the occurrence in any manner. The appellants namely Muhammad Bilal alias Mithu son of Abdul Jabbar, Liaquat Ali alias Liaqu son of Maqsood Ahmad and Aman Ullah son of Shah Muhammad opted not to get themselves examined under section 340 Cr.P.C. and did not adduce any evidence in their defence. 10. On the conclusion of the trial, the learned Additional Sessions Judge, Layyah, convicted and sentenced the appellants as referred to above. 11. The contention of the learned counsel for the appellants precisely is that the whole case is fabricated and false.

On the conclusion of the trial, the learned Additional Sessions Judge, Layyah, convicted and sentenced the appellants as referred to above. 11. The contention of the learned counsel for the appellants precisely is that the whole case is fabricated and false. The learned counsel for the appellants submitted that the prosecution remained unable to prove the facts in issue and did not produce any unimpeachable, admissible and relevant evidence. The learned counsel for the appellants further contended that the statements of prosecution witnesses were not worthy of any reliance. The learned counsel for the appellants also submitted that the appellants had no connection with the occurrence. The learned counsel for the appellants also argued that the recoveries were full of procedural defects, of no legal worth and value and result of fake proceedings. Facts which establish the identity of any person whose identity is relevant are, by virtue of Article 22 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat, 1984, always relevant. The term 'identification' means proving that a person before the Court is the very same that he is alleged, charged or reputed to be. Identification is almost always a matter of opinion or belief. With regard to a criminal offence, identification has a two-fold object: first, to satisfy the investigating authorities, before sending a case for trial to court, that the person arrested, but not previously known to the witnesses, was the one or those who committed the crime,; second, to satisfy the court that the accused was the real offender concerned with the crime. Identification proceedings are therefore as much in the interest of the prosecution as in the interest of the accused. An identification parade is held in the course of investigation of an offence for the purpose of enabling the witnesses to identify the persons who are concerned with the offence; they are not held merely for the purpose of identifying persons irrespective of their connection with the offence; the witnesses are explained the purpose of holding these parades and are asked to identify the persons which are concerned in the offence. But it is obvious that if before the court a witness pointed to a stranger and stated that he was the offender, there would be no guarantee of the truth of his assertion. Consequently, in order to have some assurance of truth, a test identification is held, that is to say, the witness at an earlier stage is confronted with the alleged offender, not standing alone but mixed with a number of innocent persons of the same age-group and of similar build and features.

But it is obvious that if before the court a witness pointed to a stranger and stated that he was the offender, there would be no guarantee of the truth of his assertion. Consequently, in order to have some assurance of truth, a test identification is held, that is to say, the witness at an earlier stage is confronted with the alleged offender, not standing alone but mixed with a number of innocent persons of the same age-group and of similar build and features. That is to say, it is to give credence to the evidence of a witness who does not know the accused from before, subsequent to the commission of the offence, that a test identification is 15 Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 111-J of 2018. held, since, without it, the evidence of the witness concerned would have little value. Of course, the substantive evidence, i.e., evidence on which alone the court can base its order of conviction or acquittal, is that given by the witness before the court, but the value of deposition of a witness there of having identified the accused in the act of the crime is of little consequence. Before the Court can accept such identification as sufficient to establish the identity of the accused, it is very necessary that there be reliable corroborative evidence, and the corroborative evidence which the Court is entitled to accept in such cases is that of a test identification parade conducted with due precautions. In short, a test identification is designed to furnish evidence to corroborate the evidence which the witness concerned tenders before the court. But of all evidence of fact, evidence about the identification of a stranger is perhaps the most elusive, and the Courts are generally agreed that the evidence of identification of a stranger based on a personal impression, even if the veracity of the witness is above board, should be approached with considerable caution, because a variety of conditions must be fulfilled before evidence based on the impression can become worthy of credence.

The State P L D 1958 SC (Pak.) 1. It has no independent value of its own Muhammad Afzal and another v. The State 1982 S C M R 129 and cannot as a rule, form a sufficient basis for conviction though the same may add some weight to the other evidence available on record Sudhindranath v. The State A I R 1952 Cal. 423 18. The identification parades (as they are normally called) are necessary only where the offender was a complete stranger to the witnesses Ismail and another v. The State 1974 S C M R 175. And the whole object of the identification proceedings is to find out whether the suspect was or was not the real offender Satya Narain v. The State A I R 1953 All. 385 and Kind v. Christle 1914 AC 545. 19. Such like identification proceedings are not the testimony of a witness but the testimony of the senses of the witness. It is essentially a test of his power of observation and perception, a test of his power to recognize strangers and a test of his memory. These gifts of God may vary from man to man. A witness may be honest, independent and truthful but then his memory may be faulty. And then the tricks of memory and its conscious and unconscious activity could also wrap the vision of a man. When mistakes are possible in the recognition of a man known from before, then the possibility of such mistakes in identifying strangers is definitely greater. And more so when the witnesses have seen the offender for the first time during the occurrence and that also briefly and not with a calm but in an excited, confused and terrorised state of mind. 20.

What then are the standards required to be satisfied by such an evidence before the same could be accepted by a Court of law? 22. The answer is that the vital factor determinative of the worth and value of identification proceedings is the effectiveness of the precautions taken, before and during the course of such proceedings which are designed to eliminate the possibility of unjustified convictions. 23. Although there is no law, which prescribes any such precautions yet the necessary guidelines are available in the form of executive instructions and judicial pronouncements. Some of them are summarised as under:- (a) Memories fade and visions get blurred with passage of time. Thus, an identification test, where an unexplained and unreasonably long period has intervened between the occurrence and the identification proceedings, should be viewed with suspicion.

Some of them are summarised as under:- (a) Memories fade and visions get blurred with passage of time. Thus, an identification test, where an unexplained and unreasonably long period has intervened between the occurrence and the identification proceedings, should be viewed with suspicion. Therefore, an' identification parade, to inspire confidence, must be held at the earliest possible opportunity after the occurrence; (b) a test identification, where the possibility of the witness having seen the accused persons after their arrest cannot be ruled out, is worth nothing at all. It is, therefore, imperative to eliminate all such possibilities. It should be ensured that, after their arrest, the suspects are put to identification tests as early as possible. Such suspects should preferably, not be remanded to police custody in the first instance and should be kept in judicial custody till the identification proceedings are held. This is to avoid the possibility of overzealous I.Os. showing the suspects to the witnesses while they are in police custody. Even when these accused persons are, of necessity, to be taken to Courts for remand etc. they must be warned to cover their faces if they so choose so that no witness could see them; (c) identification parades should never be held at police stations; 18 Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 111-J of 2018. In the late 1960s, the courts around the world, began to set the standard for reviewing eyewitness identification evidence Reliability and credibility of the witness was termed as the linchpin in determining the admissibility of identification testimony. US Supreme Court in the case of Manson v Brathwaite, UK Court of Appeal (Criminal Division) in Regina v. Turnbull and Another, New Jersey Supreme Court in State v Madison and Oregon Supreme Court in State v. Classen settled the following factors for assessing the reliability of the witness: the opportunity of the witness to view the suspect at the time of the crime; the witness's degree of attention; the accuracy of the witness's prior description of the suspect; the level of certainty demonstrated at the confrontation (seeing the accused in court); and the time between the crime and the confrontation (seeing the accused in court). It is interesting to note that these factors were drawn from earlier judicial rulings and not from scientific research. The scientific research refutes the notion that memory is like a video recording, and that a witness needs only to replay the tape 25 Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 111-J of 2018. to remember what happened. Human memory is far more complex. The memory is a constructive, dynamic, and selective process. The process of remembering consists of three stages: acquisition-"the perception of the original event"; retention-"the period of time that passes between the event and the eventual recollection of a particular piece of information"; and retrieval-the "stage during which a person recalls stored information". The process of memory retention and retrieval may be affected by a number of factors. The scientific literature divides those variables into two categories: system and estimator variables. System variables are factors like lineup procedures which are within the control of the criminal justice system and in our jurisprudence are referred to as the Test Identification Parade.

The scientific literature divides those variables into two categories: system and estimator variables. System variables are factors like lineup procedures which are within the control of the criminal justice system and in our jurisprudence are referred to as the Test Identification Parade. Whereas Estimator variables are factors related to the witness - like distance, lighting, or stress - over which the legal system has no control. Our courts have marginally attended to this aspect of witness reliability before placing reliance on the identification evidence (see above). The scientific research establishes that the following non-exhaustive list of "estimator variables" negatively affect the memory process:- i. Stress: Even under the best viewing conditions, high levels of stress can diminish an eye-witness' ability to recall and make an accurate identification. It may be noted "while moderate levels of stress improve cognitive processing and might improve accuracy, an eye-witness under high stress is less likely to make a reliable identification of the perpetrator." ii. Weapon Focus: When a visible weapon is used during a crime, it can distract a witness and draw his or her attention away from the culprit. "Weapon focus" can thus impair a witness' ability to make a reliable identification and describe what the culprit looks like if the crime is of short duration. iii. Duration: The amount of time an eye-witness has to observe an event may affect the reliability of an identification. There is no minimum time required to make an accurate identification, however, a brief or fleeting contact is less likely to produce an accurate identification than a more prolonged exposure." iv. Distance and Lighting: A person is easier to recognize when close by, and that clarity decreases with distance. We also know that poor lighting makes it harder to see well. Thus, greater distance between a witness and a perpetrator and poor lighting conditions can diminish the reliability of an identification.27 v.

We also know that poor lighting makes it harder to see well. Thus, greater distance between a witness and a perpetrator and poor lighting conditions can diminish the reliability of an identification.27 v. Witness Characteristics: Characteristics like a witness' age and level of intoxication can affect the reliability of an identification. Children between the ages of nine and thirteen who view target-absent lineups are more likely to make incorrect identifications than adults. vi. Characteristics of Perpetrator: Disguises and changes in facial features altered between the time of the event and the identification procedure affects the accuracy of an identification. vii. Memory Decay: Memories fade with time and memory decay "is irreversible"; memories never improve. As a result, delays between the commission of a crime and the time an identification is made can affect reliability. The scientific research referred to above has not only appeared in the peer reviewed journals but also has been considered "credible" by various courts in different jurisdictions. New Jersey Supreme Court in State v. Henderson observed that "virtually all of the scientific evidence" that had emerged in recent decades "reveals that an array of variables can affect and dilute memory and lead to misidentifications." Also see State v. Lawson. 26 Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 111-J of 2018. 14. The laws of evidence maintain that in order for the court to take judicial notice of scientific facts they must be part of the general knowledge of men or must be agreed upon by reputable men in a particular field of science beyond reasonable dispute. For judges to determine the degree of consensus on a particular scientific fact they may refer to any reputable and recognized reference sources.35 The House of Lords in Regina (Quintavalle) v. Secretary of State for Health36 held that the laws have to be construed in the light of contemporary scientific knowledge and in order to give effect to a plain parliamentary purpose, the statute may be held to cover a scientific development not known when the statute was passed. This Court can take judicial notice of the credible scientific development under Article 112, Qanun-e-Shahadat, 1984. The question is can we shut our eyes to credible scientific research and development, which has already been recognized and acknowledged by the courts in various other jurisdictions. If scientific research can help and assist the court in understanding and appreciating evidence more fully and more meaningfully, the risk of miscarriage of justice stands minimized. Therefore, the courts don't shy away from scientific developments but instead reach out and embrace them. Reliance on scientific research and the factors evolved by science to assess the reliability and credibility of the eye-witness can improve the quality of identification evidence and as a consequence the quality of justice. Our jurisprudence had already travelled in this direction and now credible scientific research by providing us additional factors or "estimator variables" (which are not exhaustive) has provided additional factors to certify the credibility and reliability of the eye-witness and as a result the veracity and probative value of the identification evidence.” Estimator variables are factors related to the witness, like distance, lighting, or stress during the occurrence which factors are directly related to the capacity of a witness to first observe and then to retain the features of the accused for him to subsequently remember them with such clarity so as to make a correct identification later during the test identification parade proceedings.

The mere fact that witness is able to pick out an accused person from amongst a crowd does not prove that he has identified that accused person as having taken part in the crime which is being investigated. It merely means that the witness happens to know that accused person.. The evidence of mere identification of the accused person at the trial for the first time is from its very nature inherently of a weak character. The evidence in order to carry conviction should ordinarily clarify as to how and under what circumstances he came to pick out the particular accused person and the details of the part which the accused played in the crime in question with reasonable particularity. Such kind of test identification proceedings where the part played by the accused is not given, have no legal value. Reliance is placed upon a binding judgment of the august Supreme Court of Pakistan in the case titled “MAJEED alias MAJEEDI and others v. The State and others” (2019 S C M R 301) wherein it has been held as under: “At any rate, in the test identification parade the complainant and two other eye- witnesses had maintained that the appellant had only searched the complainant and some others during the alleged occurrence and they had not attributed any 36 Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 111-J of 2018. role to the appellant regarding causing of murder of the deceased or causing injuries to others but another eye-witness belonging to the complainant party had maintained that the appellant had also fired at the said witness causing an injury on his lip.

The prosecution has attempted to complete the chain of circumstantial evidence by medical evidence relating to the post mortem examinations of the deceased persons. This evidence proves only the factum that death of the deceased persons was caused by cutting their throats through some sharp edge weapon; it does in no way indicate who had cut 52 Murder Reference No.24 of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 109-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 110-J of 2018 Criminal Appeal No. 111-J of 2018. their throats and with what particular weapon. Medical evidence is in the nature of supporting, confirmatory or explanatory of the direct or circumstantial evidence, and is not "corroborative evidence" in the sense the term is used in legal parlance for a piece of evidence that itself also has some probative force to connect the accused person with the commission of offence. Medical evidence by itself does not throw any light on the identity of the offender. Such evidence may confirm the available substantive evidence with regard to certain facts including seat of the injury, nature of the injury, cause of the death, kind of the weapon used in the occurrence, duration between the injuries and the death, and presence of an injured witness or the injured accused at the place of occurrence, but it does not connect the accused with the commission of the offence. It cannot constitute corroboration for proving involvement of the accused person in the commission of offence, as it does not establish the identity of the accused person.32 Therefore, the medical evidence is of little help to the prosecution for bringing home the guilt to the petitioners.” 28. It is a cardinal principle of justice and law that only the intrinsic worth and probative value of the evidence would play a decisive role in determining the guilt or innocence of an accused person. Even evidence of uninterested witness, not inimical to the accused, may be corrupted deliberately while evidence of inimical witness, if found consistent with the other evidence corroborating it, may be relied upon. It is settled principle of law that for giving benefit of doubt it is not necessary that there should be so many circumstances rather if only a single circumstance creating reasonable doubt in the mind of a prudent person is available then such benefit is to be extended to an accused not as a matter of concession but as of right. The august Supreme Court of Pakistan in the case of “Muhammad Mansha Vs. The State” (2018 SCMR 772) has enunciated the following principle: “Needless to mention that while giving the benefit of doubt to an accused it is not necessary that there should be many circumstances creating doubt. If there is a circumstance which creates reasonable doubt in a prudent mind about the guilt of the accused, then the accused would be entitled to the benefit of such doubt, not as a matter of grace and concession, but as a matter of right. It is based on the maxim, "it is better that ten guilty persons be acquitted rather than one innocent person be convicted". Reliance in this behalf can be made upon the cases of Tariq Pervez v. The State (1995 SCMR 1345), Ghulam Qadir and 2 others v. The State (2008 SCMR 1221), Muhammad Akramv.The State (2009 SCMR 230) and Muhammad Zamanv.The State (2014 SCMR 749).” Reliance is also placed on the judgment of the august Supreme Court of Pakistan Najaf Ali Shah Vs. the State (2021 S C M R 736) in which it has been observed in as infra: “9. Mere heinousness of the offence if not proved to the hilt is not a ground to avail the majesty of the court to do complete justice. This is an established principle of law and equity that it is better that 100 guilty persons should let off but one innocent person should not suffer. As the preeminent English jurist William Blackstone wrote, "Better that ten guilty persons escape, than that one innocent suffer." Benjamin Franklin, who was one of the leading figures of early American history, went further arguing "it is better a hundred guilty persons should escape than one innocent person should suffer." All the contradictions noted by the learned High Court are sufficient to cast a shadow of doubt on the prosecution's case, which entitles the petitioner to the right of benefit of the doubt.
